course_info.title: Data Science and Analytics
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Data Science and the Analytics Pipeline
learning_objectives: Understand the end-to-end data science workflow
Set up a Python data science environment with pandas and Jupyter
Perform exploratory data analysis on a real dataset
readings: McKinney, Python for Data Analysis, Ch. 1-4
Wickham, R for Data Science, Ch. 1 (for the tidy data philosophy)

lecture_notes.introduction: Data science turns raw data into actionable insights. This course teaches the full pipeline: acquiring data, cleaning it, exploring it, modeling it, and communicating findings. You will work with real messy datasets, not textbook clean ones.
key_concepts: Data science pipeline: acquire, clean, explore, model, communicate
Pandas DataFrame: the workhorse data structure for tabular data
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): summary statistics, distributions, correlations
Data quality issues: missing values, duplicates, inconsistent formats
Tidy data principles: each variable is a column, each observation is a row
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a messy real-world CSV dataset into pandas, identify data quality issues, clean them, compute summary statistics, and create initial visualizations
setup_requirements: Python 3.12+ with pandas, numpy, matplotlib, seaborn

review.value: A dataset is tidy when each variable has its own column, each observation has its own row, and each value has its own cell; most data wrangling effort goes into getting data into tidy form

Always explore data before building models; visualizations reveal patterns, outliers, and data quality issues that summary statistics miss. A model built on data you have not explored is built on sand

learning_objectives: Transform messy data into analysis-ready format using pandas
Engineer informative features from raw data
Handle missing data with appropriate imputation strategies
readings: McKinney, Ch. 7-8 - Data Cleaning and Wrangling
Feature Engineering for Machine Learning, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Data scientists spend 80% of their time cleaning and transforming data. Feature engineering — creating new variables from existing ones — is often the difference between a mediocre model and an excellent one.
key_concepts: Pandas operations: merge, join, groupby, pivot, melt
String manipulation and datetime parsing
Missing data strategies: drop, fill forward, mean impute, model-based imputation
Feature engineering: binning, encoding categoricals, interaction terms, lag features
Data validation: asserting data quality invariants programmatically
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a raw e-commerce dataset with multiple tables, join them, handle missing values, engineer features (days since last purchase, average order value, purchase frequency), and validate the result

review.value: Convert categorical variables to binary columns (one per category); this is necessary for algorithms that require numeric input. Use drop_first=True to avoid the dummy variable trap in linear models

Features that contain information about the target that would not be available at prediction time; this causes artificially high accuracy during training but fails in production. Always verify feature availability at prediction time

learning_objectives: Conduct hypothesis tests for comparing groups and detecting relationships
Interpret p-values, confidence intervals, and effect sizes correctly
Avoid common statistical pitfalls: multiple testing, p-hacking

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistical analysis provides the rigor that separates data science from data anecdotes. Hypothesis testing tells you whether observed patterns are likely real or just noise. Understanding statistics is essential for making defensible data-driven decisions.
key_concepts: Null and alternative hypotheses
t-tests: comparing two group means
Chi-square test: testing independence of categorical variables
Correlation vs causation: observational data limitations
Multiple testing correction: Bonferroni, FDR
Effect size: practical significance beyond statistical significance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze an A/B test dataset: formulate hypotheses, conduct a t-test, calculate confidence intervals and effect size, and make a recommendation accounting for both statistical and practical significance

review.value: The p-value is NOT the probability that the null hypothesis is true; it is the probability of observing data this extreme if the null hypothesis were true. A small p-value means the data is unlikely under H0, not that H0 is false

A statistically significant result with a tiny effect size may not be practically important; always report effect size alongside p-values. With large enough samples, any trivial difference becomes statistically significant

learning_objectives: Create effective visualizations using matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly
Design dashboards that communicate insights to stakeholders
Apply data storytelling principles to present findings persuasively
readings: Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The best analysis is useless if stakeholders do not understand or act on it. Data visualization and storytelling are how you translate complex findings into decisions. A good chart communicates in seconds what a table of numbers cannot communicate at all.
key_concepts: Chart selection: when to use bar, line, scatter, box, heatmap
Decluttering: removing chart junk to focus attention on data
Color usage: sequential, diverging, and categorical palettes
Interactive dashboards with plotly and Streamlit
Narrative structure: situation, complication, resolution for data stories
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take an EDA analysis and transform it into a stakeholder presentation: replace exploratory charts with publication-quality visuals, add context and narrative, and build an interactive dashboard
setup_requirements: Python with matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, streamlit

review.value: Size, color, position, and orientation are processed by the brain before conscious thought; use these strategically to draw attention to the key insight in your chart without requiring the viewer to study it
Maximize the proportion of ink used to display data vs non-data elements; remove gridlines, excessive labels, 3D effects, and decorative elements that do not carry information

class_schedule.topic: Big Data Technologies and Scalable Analytics
learning_objectives: Understand distributed computing frameworks for big data
Write PySpark jobs for data processing at scale
Identify when big data tools are needed vs when pandas suffices
readings: Google, 'MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Datasets' (2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: When your data exceeds the memory of a single machine, you need distributed processing. Spark and similar frameworks let you apply the same analytical thinking to terabytes that you apply to megabytes, but with different performance trade-offs.
key_concepts: MapReduce paradigm: map (transform), shuffle (redistribute), reduce (aggregate)
Apache Spark: RDDs, DataFrames, lazy evaluation, in-memory processing
PySpark: Spark with Python API, SQL interface
Partitioning and shuffling: where performance bottlenecks live
When to use Spark vs pandas: the 10GB rule of thumb
lecture_notes.demonstration: Process a 5GB web log dataset with PySpark: read from S3, filter, aggregate page views by hour and country, and write results to Parquet format, all in a Jupyter notebook connected to a Spark cluster

review.value: Spark builds a computation graph (DAG) but does not execute until an action (collect, write, count) is called; this enables optimization like predicate pushdown and column pruning before any data is processed

Shuffling data across nodes (during groupBy, join, sort) is the most expensive operation in distributed processing; minimizing shuffles through proper partitioning and broadcast joins is the key to Spark performance

questions.question: Given a DataFrame with columns [user_id, purchase_date, amount, category], write pandas code to calculate the total spend per user per month, then find the top 5 users by total spend.
questions.answer:
df['month'] = df['purchase_date'].dt.to_period('M')
monthly_spend = df.groupby(['user_id', 'month'])['amount'].sum().reset_index()
top_users = df.groupby('user_id')['amount'].sum().nlargest(5)
# Or combined:
top_users = df.groupby('user_id')['amount'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)

questions.question: A dataset has 15% missing values in a numeric column. Describe three different imputation strategies and when each is appropriate.
questions.answer:
1. Mean/median imputation: Replace missing values with the column mean (median for skewed data). Appropriate when data is missing completely at random and the column distribution is roughly normal. Fast but reduces variance and can bias correlations.
2. Forward fill: Replace missing values with the previous non-missing value. Appropriate for time series data where the last known value is a reasonable estimate.
3. Model-based imputation (e.g., KNN or iterative imputer): Use other features to predict missing values. Appropriate when missingness depends on other variables (MAR) and when preserving multivariate relationships matters. Most accurate but most complex.

rubric.key: Three distinct strategies named and described

questions.question: An A/B test comparing a new checkout flow (B) against the original (A) shows: A conversion rate = 3.2%, B conversion rate = 3.8%, p-value = 0.04, sample size = 50,000 per group. Should the team launch variant B? Justify your answer considering both statistical and practical significance.
questions.answer: The p-value of 0.04 < 0.05 suggests the difference is statistically significant. However, the absolute lift is only 0.6 percentage points (3.8% - 3.2%). Before launching, consider: (1) Is a 0.6pp lift practically significant for the business? At 50K users, this is 300 additional conversions. (2) Calculate the 95% confidence interval for the lift to understand the range of plausible effect sizes. (3) Check the effect size (Cohen's h or relative lift of 18.75%). (4) Consider the cost of implementing and maintaining the new flow. If 300 additional conversions per period is valuable and the implementation cost is low, launch B. If the margin of error includes zero or the lift is too small to justify the engineering cost, consider running the test longer for a more precise estimate.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets p-value and statistical significance
Considers practical significance and business impact

questions.question: Explain why a bar chart is more appropriate than a pie chart for comparing 8 categories, and when a pie chart might be acceptable.
questions.answer: Bar charts use position along a common axis to encode value, which the human visual system compares more accurately than the angles and areas used by pie charts. With 8 categories, pie slices become too small to compare reliably, and similar-sized slices are nearly indistinguishable. Bar charts also support easy sorting (largest to smallest) and comparison across charts. A pie chart is acceptable only when: (1) there are 2-3 categories, (2) the goal is to show parts of a whole rather than compare values, and (3) one category dominates (e.g., 70/20/10 split).

rubric.key: Explains position encoding is more accurate than angle
Notes difficulty comparing many small pie slices

major_assignments.title: End-to-End Data Science Project
major_assignments.description: Conduct a complete data science project on a real-world dataset: from data acquisition and cleaning through analysis and modeling to a final presentation. Your analysis must answer a specific, well-defined question with evidence-based conclusions.
objectives: Formulate a clear analytical question and design a methodology to answer it
Clean, explore, and engineer features from a messy real-world dataset
Apply appropriate statistical tests and build predictive models
Communicate findings through visualizations and a stakeholder presentation
deliverables: Jupyter Notebook with complete reproducible analysis
Stakeholder presentation (10 slides) summarizing findings and recommendations
Analysis report (4-5 pages) with methodology, findings, and limitations

criteria: Data cleaning is thorough and well-documented
Missing data handled with appropriate strategies
Feature engineering creates informative variables
Data quality is validated programmatically
Statistical tests are appropriate for the question
Results are interpreted correctly with effect sizes
Modeling approach is justified and properly validated
Limitations are acknowledged honestly
Charts are appropriate for the data type and message
Visualizations follow best practices (decluttered, labeled, accessible)
Interactive dashboard provides useful exploration capability
Data story has a clear narrative arc
Stakeholder presentation is clear and actionable
Technical depth is appropriate for the audience
Methodology is reproducible from the notebook
Report is well-written and professionally formatted
Complete data science project with rigorous methodology
Accurate analysis with appropriate statistical rigor
Effective visualizations and stakeholder communication
Professional documentation with honest limitations

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of data science concepts

midterm_examination.description: Written exam on statistics and data wrangling

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all data science topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for lectures and mandatory for lab sessions.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of analytical approaches is encouraged. All analysis and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying analysis code from Kaggle or other sources without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Python 3.12+ with pandas, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, plotly, and Jupyter. PySpark will be used in the final unit.
